title: 第 13 節
en: There are but few chronological sheet-anchors which hold the ancient history of India, and we must try to fasten the floating literature of the Veda to one of them, as firmly and securel}^ as we can. In order to do that I must, however, first say a few words more on another class of literary compositions which form the last products of the Vedic age, and which will have to serve as our hawsers to connect the ancient history of India with the terra firma of Greek chronology.
The Sutras.
If you could read some of the Brahma^ias, which I ^ See Appendix IX.

AGE OF THE VEDA. 89 described to you in my last lecture, you would easily understand why, even for tlie purposes for which they were principally intended, they proved in the long run utterly useless. I defy any one to learn the correct performance of a Vedic sacrifice from these treatises. This explains the rise of a new kind of literature, in style the very opposite of the BrahmaTias, in which the performance of the same sacrifices which we saw described in theBrahma7/as, is explained in the shortest and the most business-like manner. These works are called Sutra, which means literally threads. Some passages occurring in the Brahma?ias and containing short rules are called by the same name, and it is quite clear that these Sutras, though independent works, are entirely based on ancient Brahma72as. Their style is almost enigmatical by its terseness, their grammar retains but few traces of the Vedic language, though Vedic irregularities are tolerated in them, while the language of the Brahma7ias is still entirely Vedic, and contains many ancient forms, even such as do not occur in the Vedic hymns.

The introduction of this new class of literature must have been the result of some social or religious change. The change from the careless diffuseness of the Brahma^ias to the studied brevity of the Sutras must have had a definite purpose.

I can think of two explanations only. It is just possible that a knowledge of the art of writing, which was unknown to the authors of the Brahmarms, may have reached India sooner than we know, and that its inherent difficulties may have produced at first this almost lapidary style of the Sutras. What is against this supposition is the non-ap- 90 LECTURE V.
pearance of any allusion to writing in the Sutras themselves.

We are therefore driven to the other explanation, that the Brahmans themselves could no longer trust to a traditional knowledge of the different sacrifices ; that the text of the Brahma^ias, even if learnt by heart, was no longer found sufficient to enable priests to perform their respective duties correctly, and that therefore these new practical manuals were composed, containing no useless speculations, but simply an outline of the duties of the three classes of priests, a thread of rules to be learnt by heart by the priests who had to perform the sacrifices.

These Sutras are called Kalpa-sutras, and are divided into two classes, ^'rauta and Smarta. ♦Sfrauta ivS derived from sruti, hearing, which means revelation, Smarta is derived from smriti, memory, which means tradition.
Each class of priests, the labouring, the singing, and the reciting priests, have their own Sutras, as they had their own Brahma ?ias and Sar^ihitas.

When this Sutra-style had once become popular, other subjects also were treated in it. The rules of pronunciation, for instance, which were at first taught in metrical form, as in the Rig-veda-pratisakhya, were afterwards reduced to the form of Sutras. The rules of metre also were composed in Sutras, and not only does the Sutra-style prevail in the great grammar ascribed to Pardni, but the quotations from earlier grammarians also seem to indicate that they were handed down in the same short, pithy sentences.
AGE OF THE VEDA. 91 The Three Literary Periods of the Vedic Ag-e.

We have now finished our survey of the ancient literature of India, as it passes through three distinct stages, each marked by its own style. We saw Vedic Sanskrit at first in the metrical hymns of the Rigveda ; w^e saw it afterwards in the diffuse prose of the Brahma?ias, and we saw it last of all in the straitjacket of the Sutras.
We also saw that the Sutras presupposed the existence of the Brahmaria literature, and that the Brahma?m literature presupposed the existence of the hymns as collected in the Rig-veda-sa'J7ihita.

If now we ask how we can fix the date of these three periods, it is quite clear that we cannot hope to fix a terininiLs a quo. Whether the Vedic hymns were composed ICOO, or 1500, or 2000, or 3000 years B. c, no power on earth will ever determine.
Ghronolog'ical terminus ad quem.
The question then arises, can we fix on a terminus ad quem, can we determine the date of the last Vedic period, that of th^ Sutras, and then work our way back to the two preceding literary periods ?
Sandrocottus, died 291 B.C.

I believe this is possible. You know that the sheet-anchor of ancient Indian chronology is the date of the contemporary of Alexander the Great, Sandrocottus, who is the A'andragupta of Indian history. You may also know that this Sandrocottus, who died 291 B. c, was the grandfather of Asoka, who reigned from 259 to 222 B.C., and whose inscriptions we 92 LECTURE V.

possess eDgraved on rocks and pillars in numerous places in India. This A6oka tolerated, or even accepted the religion founded by Buddha, and it was during his reign that the second great Buddhist Council was held at Pa^aliputra.

On the strength of the information contained in the Buddhist Canon, as settled at the Council under A8oka, we are enabled to place the rise of Buddhism at about 500 B, c, and the death of its founder at 477 B. 0.
These are dates as certain in the eyes of the general historian as we can ever expect to extract from the extant literature of India.
Buddhism, a reaction ag-ainst the Vedic Beligion.

Now Buddhism is not a completely new religion. On the contrary, it represents a reaction against some other already existing religion, and more particularly against some of the extravagant theories of the Brahmans. In one sense it may really be said to be a practical carrying out of the theories, proclaimed for the first time in the Ara'nyakas and Upanishads. While the Brahmans allowed members of the three upper castes to retire from the world after they had performed all the duties of their youth and manhood, the Buddhists allowed everybody to become a Bhikshu, a mendicant, whether he had passed this previous apprenticeship or not. Again, while the Brahmans reserved the right of teaching to themselves, Buddha, who belonged to the caste of the nobles, claimed that right for himself, and for aU who were ' enlightened,' i. e. buddha. These are two essential points of difference between Brahmans and Buddhists, and AGE OF THE VEDA. 93 orthodox Brahmans constantly harp on them as proving the heterodoxy of Buddha.

But we can not only show that Buddhism was a kind of Protestantism, as compared with Brahmanism, we can point out also a number of words and thoughts, the growth of which we can watch in the periods of Vedic literature, and which were taken over bodily by the Buddhists, though sometimes with a change of meaning.
The word Upanisliad.

For instance, the very name of Upanishad can have been formed and can have grown up towards the end of the Brahmana period only. Its original meaning was a sitting (sad), below (ni), towards (up a) the teacher ^. It became the recognised name of the attitude assumed by the pupil when listening to his teacher. It then was fixed as the name of the teaching itself, and at last conveyed the meaning of secret doctrine (adesa). In that sense which it had slowly acquired in the Brahma?ia and Sutra periods, we find it used again in the sacred canon of the Southern Buddhists, who use upanisa in the sense of secret and cause. The Northern Buddhists also knew the word upanishad^. We may safely conclude therefore that this title and what it signified must have existed previous to the rise of Buddhism, that is, previous to 500 B. c.

1 5. B. E., vol. i. p. Ixxix seq. In Pali also the verb upa-ni-sad occurs with reference to a king and his friends seating themselves at the feet of a teacher. See Mahavansa, p. 82 ; Childcrs, Pali Dietionanj, s. v.
^ Var/raA7iedika, § 16, p 35 ; § 24, p. 42. There it seems to mean approach, comijarison.
The word Sutra.
zh: 印度古代歷史的時間標記少之又少，我們必須盡可能牢固地將吠陀浮動的文獻繫留於其中之一。為了做到這一點，我必須先就另一類文學作品多說幾句話。這些作品構成吠陀時代的最後產物，將作為我們的纜繩，用以連接印度古代歷史與希臘年代學的堅實大地。
蘇特拉經
如果你能夠閱讀一些梵書（Brāhmaṇas）[^1] [^1]: 見附錄第九。

吠陀時代。89 如我在上一次講座所述，你將輕易理解為何即使為了它們的主要目的，這些論著在長期而言也證明了完全無用。我敢說沒有人能從這些論著中學習到吠陀祭祀的正確執行方式。這解釋了一類全新文獻的興起，其風格與梵書（Brahma7ias）恰恰相反，以最簡潔、最務實的方式說明了我們在梵書中所見描述的同樣祭祀的執行方法。這些著作稱為蘇特羅（Sutra），字面意思為「綱要」。梵書中出現的某些包含簡短規則的段落亦以同名稱呼，而這些蘇特羅雖為獨立著作，卻完全基於上古梵書。其風格以簡潔見長，幾乎趨於謎樣；其文法僅保留吠陀語言的寥寥痕跡，儘管吠陀的不規則用法在其中被容許，而梵書的語言仍然完全是吠陀語言，且包含許多上古詞彙，甚至連那些不出現於吠陀頌歌的詞彙亦然。
這類新文獻的引入必然是某種社會或宗教變化的結果。從《梵書》（Brahmanas）的漫不經心之冗長蛻變為《經頌》（Sutras）的精心簡潔，必然有其明確的目的。
我只能想到兩種解釋。有可能掌握書寫藝術的知識——這對吠陀經（Brahmanas）的作者來說是陌生的——比我們所知更早傳入印度，而其固有的困難可能最初產生了蘇特拉（Sutras）這種幾乎像銘刻體的風格。反對這一假設的是
在《經文》(Sutras)本身中找不到任何關於文字的隻言片語。
因此我們被迫接受另一種解釋：婆羅門本身已不再信任對各種祭祀的傳統知識；即使《梵書》（Brāhmaṇas）已熟記於心，亦不足以使祭司們能正確地履行各自的職責，因此才編纂了這些新的實用手冊，其中不包含無謂的思辨，而只是三個祭司階級職責的大綱，以及應由執行祭祀的祭司熟記於心的規則線索。
這些經文稱為「劫波經」（Kalpa-sutras），分為兩類：祀火經（śrauta）和家庭經（smarta）。祀火經來自「聞」（śruti），意為啟示；家庭經來自「憶」（smṛti），意為傳統。
每一類祭司——勞動祭司、歌唱祭司及誦經祭司——都有各自的經軌（Sutras），正如他們各自擁有梵書（Brahma?ias）和本集（Sar?ihitas）一樣。
一旦經文體（Sutra-style）風行起來，其他科目也採取了這種形式。例如發音規則最初是以韻文形式教授的，如在《梨俱吠陀護衛書》（Rig-veda-pratisakhya）中所見，後來便被簡化為經文形式。韻律規則也以經文形式編訂，不僅帕尼尼（Pardni）所著的大法典採用了經文體，而且早期文法學家的引文也表明他們的著作是以同樣簡潔有力的句子形式流傳下來的。
吠陀時代 91 吠陀時代的三個文學時期
我們現已完成對古代印度文獻的概覽，它經歷了三個截然不同的階段，每個階段都有其獨特的風格。我們首先在《梨俱吠陀》的韻文讚歌中見到吠陀梵文；其後在《梵書》冗長的散文中見到它；最後在《經文》（Sutra）的嚴格束縛中見到它。
我們也看到經典（Sutra）預設了婆羅門文獻的存在，而婆羅門文獻則預設了以《梨俱吠陀‧本集》（Rig-veda-sa'hita）匯集的讚歌的存在。
如果我們現在問起如何確定這三個時期的年代，很顯然我們不能期待確定一個起始的終點（terminus a quo）。無論吠陀讚歌是在公元前一千年、一千五百年、二千年或三千年創作的，世間任何力量都永遠無法確定。
時間上之終極界限。
於是問題隨之而起：我們能否確定一個終點（terminus ad quem），能否定出最後吠陀時期的年代，即蘇特羅經（Sutra）的年代，然後向前推溯至前面兩個文獻時期？
桑德羅科圖斯（Sandrocottus），卒於西元前291年。
我相信這是可能的。您知道古代印度年代學的堅實基礎是亞歷山大大帝的同時代人散德羅科土斯（Sandrocottus），他就是印度歷史上的月護王（A'andragupta）。您可能也知道，這位散德羅科土斯於西元前291年去世，是阿育王的祖父，而阿育王統治時期為西元前259年至222年，他的銘文我們 第92頁 第五講。
刻在印度各地岩石和柱子上的碑文。阿育王（Aśoka）寬容，甚至接納了佛陀所創立的宗教，在他的統治期間，第二次大佛教理事會在華氏城（Pāṭaliputra）召開。
根據阿育王（Aśoka）委任之議會所確立的《佛教經藏》（Buddhist Canon）所載之資料，我們得以將佛教的興起定年於西元前約五百年，其創始人的逝世定年於西元前四百七十七年。
這些年代對於一般歷史學家而言，乃是我們所能從現存印度文獻中發掘出來最為確定的日期。
佛教，對吠陀宗教的反動。

現在，佛教並非完全嶄新的宗教。相反地，它代表對某種既已存在的宗教的反動，尤其是對婆羅門的某些過度理論的反動。在某種意義上，它確實可以說是最初在《森林奧義書》（Ara'nyakas）和《奧義書》中宣揚的理論的實踐執行。婆羅門允許三個上等種姓的成員在履行完青年和壯年時期的所有義務後隱居塵外，而佛教徒則允許任何人成為比丘（Bhikshu），一位托缽者，無論他是否經歷過這種先前的修習。同樣地，婆羅門將教法權保留給自己，而屬於貴族種姓的佛陀為自己和所有「開悟」的人（即佛陀）聲稱此項權利。這是婆羅門與佛教徒之間的兩個本質性差異，而正統婆羅門不斷強調這些差異，以此證明佛陀的異端性。
但我們不僅能夠指出佛教相較於婆羅門教是一種新教運動，我們還可以指出許多詞彙和思想，其發展過程我們可以在吠陀文獻的各個時期裡加以追蹤，而這些詞彙和思想被佛教徒原封不動地承襲下來，儘管有時其含義會有所改變。
奧義書一詞。

例如，「奧義書」(Upanishad)這個名稱本身就只能是在婆羅門時代末期才形成並發展起來的。它的原始含義是「坐」(sad)、「下」(ni)、「朝向」(up a)老師。它後來成為學生聆聽老師時所採取之姿態的公認名稱。隨後它被確立為教導本身的名稱，最終傳達出秘密教義(adesa)的意涵。在南傳佛教的聖典中，我們發現奧義書以它在婆羅門時代和經文時代(Sutra periods)緩慢獲得的含義被使用，南傳佛教徒使用 upanisa 一詞以表示秘密與因果的意思。北傳佛教徒也知道「奧義書」(upanishad)這個詞。因此我們可以安全地得出結論：這個標題及其所代表的含義必然存在於佛教興起之前，即公元前 500 年之前。
1 5. B. E., vol. i. p. lxxix seq. 在巴利文中，動詞 upa-ni-sad 亦出現於描述國王及其隨從坐在教師足前的語境中。參見 Mahavansa, p. 82；Childers, Pali Dictionary, s. v.
^ Var/raA7iedika, § 16, p 35 ; § 24, p. 42。在此處似乎表示接近、比較。

蘇特拉（*sutra*）一詞在梵文中的原義為「線」或「縷」。後來它被用來指稱簡潔的格言或箴言，特別是指宗教與哲學的簡短教義。在印度文學中，蘇特拉體裁的著作通常以最濃縮的形式呈現教義，力求用最少的言詞表達最多的內涵。 蘇特拉文獻在印度精神傳統中佔有重要地位。它們常見於各類宗教與哲學學派，尤其在婆羅門教與佛教系統中。這類著作的特點在於其簡潔性與象徵性，往往需要教師的口頭解釋與闡發，方能為學生所領悟。 蘇特拉體裁的使用反映了印度思想傳統中「言簡意賅」的美學理想。每一條蘇特拉都被視為智慧的濃縮精華，學者與修行者透過反覆咀嚼與冥想，得以從中汲取深刻的精神內涵。
